definition: A substance that increases the rate of a chemical reaction without itself being permanently changed. It provides an alternative reaction pathway with a lower activation energy.
example: MnO₂ catalyses the decomposition of H₂O₂ into H₂O and O₂.

definition: The minimum energy that colliding particles must possess for a reaction to occur.
example: A catalyst lowers Ea so more particles can react at a given temperature.

definition: A reaction occurs when particles collide with sufficient energy (≥ Ea) and the correct orientation. Increasing concentration, temperature or surface area increases the collision frequency and thus the rate.
example: Fine MnO₂ powder reacts faster than lumps because it has greater surface area.

definition: The power to which a reactant's concentration is raised in the rate equation. A first-order reaction has rate ∝ [A]; shown by a straight line through the origin on a rate vs concentration graph.
example: The Na₂S₂O₃ / HCl reaction is first-order with respect to Na₂S₂O₃.

definition: A dynamic state in a reversible reaction where the rate of the forward reaction equals the rate of the reverse reaction, and the concentrations of reactants and products remain constant.
example: H₂(g) + I₂(g) ⇌ 2HI(g) reaches a state where all three species coexist at constant concentrations.

definition: When a system at equilibrium is subjected to a change (stress), the system responds in a way that minimises that change.
example: Adding more CO to CO + 2H₂ ⇌ CH₃OH shifts equilibrium to the right, increasing methanol yield.

definition: The ratio of the product of the molar concentrations of the products to those of the reactants, each raised to the power of their stoichiometric coefficients, at a given temperature.
example: For aA + bB ⇌ cC + dD: Kc = [C]ᶜ[D]ᵈ / [A]ᵃ[B]ᵇ

definition: Kc only changes when temperature changes. It does NOT change with concentration, pressure, or catalyst. For an exothermic reaction, increasing temperature decreases Kc (shifts equilibrium left).
example: In CO + 2H₂ ⇌ CH₃OH (exothermic), raising temperature reduces Kc and reduces methanol yield.

definition: An acid that completely (fully) dissociates into ions in aqueous solution.
example: HCl → H⁺ + Cl⁻ (100% dissociation). Other examples: H₂SO₄, HNO₃.

definition: An acid that only partially dissociates into ions in aqueous solution, establishing an equilibrium.
example: CH₃COOH ⇌ CH₃COO⁻ + H⁺ (only ~1% dissociated). Other examples: HF, carbonic acid.

definition: pH = −log₁₀[H⁺]. A measure of the acidity or alkalinity of a solution. pH < 7 is acidic, pH = 7 is neutral, pH > 7 is alkaline.
A substance used to prepare a standard solution of accurately known concentration. Properties: high purity, high molar mass (reduces weighing errors), stable, does not absorb moisture.
example: (NH₄)₂SO₄·FeSO₄·6H₂O (Mr = 392) is a primary standard for redox titrations.

definition: A weak acid or base that changes colour at a specific pH range, used to detect the endpoint of a titration.
example: Phenolphthalein: colourless in acid, pink in base (range pH 8–10). Used for strong base/weak acid titrations.

definition: The number of protons in the nucleus of an atom. It defines the element and equals the number of electrons in a neutral atom.
example: Carbon has atomic number 6 (6 protons, 6 electrons in neutral atom).

definition: Atoms of the same element that have the same atomic number (same number of protons) but different mass numbers (different number of neutrons).
example: Carbon-12 (⁶¹²C) and Carbon-14 (⁶¹⁴C) are isotopes — both have 6 protons but different neutrons.

definition: The weighted average mass of all naturally occurring isotopes of an element, expressed relative to 1/12 the mass of a carbon-12 atom.
example: Chlorine Ar = 35.5, because 75% is ³⁵Cl and 25% is ³⁷Cl: (0.75×35 + 0.25×37) = 35.5.

definition: Electrons fill atomic orbitals in order of increasing energy, filling lower-energy orbitals before higher ones.
example: 1s fills before 2s, 2s before 2p, 3s before 3p before 3d, etc.

definition: Electrons occupy separate orbitals within the same subshell (with parallel spins) before pairing up in the same orbital.
example: In the 2p subshell with 3 electrons: each of the three 2p orbitals gets one electron before any pairing.

definition: No two electrons in the same atom can have the same four quantum numbers. Each orbital can hold a maximum of two electrons with opposite spins.
example: The 1s orbital can hold at most 2 electrons, one spin-up and one spin-down.

definition: The minimum energy required to remove one mole of electrons from one mole of gaseous atoms in their ground state.
example: Na(g) → Na⁺(g) + e⁻; ΔH = +496 kJ mol⁻¹. Generally increases across Period 3 with exceptions at Mg→Al and P→S.

definition: The heat energy change at constant pressure. Exothermic reactions have ΔH < 0 (heat released); endothermic reactions have ΔH > 0 (heat absorbed).
example: Neutralisation: H⁺(aq) + OH⁻(aq) → H₂O(l); ΔH ≈ −57 kJ mol⁻¹ (exothermic).

definition: The formula for calculating heat energy transferred. Q = heat (J), m = mass of solution (g), c = specific heat capacity (J g⁻¹ K⁻¹), ΔT = temperature change (°C or K).
example: 50 g solution rises by 6.7°C: Q = 50 × 4.18 × 6.7 = 1400 J = 1.40 kJ.

definition: The enthalpy change of a reaction is the same regardless of the route taken, provided the initial and final conditions are the same.
example: C + O₂ → CO₂ has the same ΔH whether done directly or via CO as an intermediate step.

definition: The energy required to break one mole of a particular covalent bond in gaseous molecules. Breaking bonds is endothermic; forming bonds is exothermic.
example: ΔH reaction = Σ(bond energies broken) − Σ(bond energies formed).

definition: The decomposition of a compound using an electric current. Oxidation occurs at the anode (positive electrode); reduction occurs at the cathode (negative electrode).
example: Electrolysis of CuSO₄ with Cu electrodes: Cu deposits at cathode, Cu dissolves from anode.

definition: An electrochemical cell in which a spontaneous chemical reaction produces electrical energy (electricity). Also called a voltaic cell.
example: A simple Daniell cell uses Zn and Cu electrodes in their respective sulfate solutions.

definition: Oxidation Is Loss (of electrons); Reduction Is Gain (of electrons). In a redox reaction, both always occur simultaneously.
example: Zn → Zn²⁺ + 2e⁻ (oxidation); Cu²⁺ + 2e⁻ → Cu (reduction).

definition: The mass of a substance deposited during electrolysis is proportional to the charge passed. Charge (C) = Current (A) × Time (s). Moles of electrons = Charge ÷ Faraday constant (96,500 C/mol).
A saturated hydrocarbon with the general formula CₙH₂ₙ₊₂. Contains only C–C single bonds. Relatively unreactive but undergoes combustion and substitution reactions.
example: Methane (CH₄), ethane (C₂H₆), propane (C₃H₈). Combustion: CH₄ + 2O₂ → CO₂ + 2H₂O.

definition: An unsaturated hydrocarbon with the general formula CₙH₂ₙ. Contains at least one C=C double bond. Undergoes addition reactions (decolourises bromine water).
example: Ethene (C₂H₄): C₂H₄ + Br₂ → C₂H₄Br₂ (addition of bromine — orange to colourless).

definition: An organic compound containing the –COOH functional group. It is weakly acidic — the O–H bond is polar, releasing H⁺ in water. Reacts with bases, alcohols, and metals.
example: Ethanoic acid (CH₃COOH): found in vinegar. Reacts with NaOH: CH₃COOH + NaOH → CH₃COONa + H₂O.

definition: Formed when a carboxylic acid reacts with an alcohol in the presence of an acid catalyst (esterification). Has the –COO– functional group. Esters have characteristic fruity smells.
example: Ethanol + ethanoic acid → ethyl ethanoate + water. Used as solvents and flavourings.

definition: A purification technique that exploits different solubilities at different temperatures. The impure solid is dissolved in hot solvent; the solution is filtered hot (to remove insoluble impurities); then cooled to crystallise the pure product.
example: Benzoic acid is purified using water: dissolve in hot water, hot-filter, cool, filter crystals.

definition: Ammoniacal silver nitrate solution. Used to test for aldehydes — a silver mirror forms on the test tube wall if an aldehyde is present (aldehyde is oxidised; Ag⁺ reduced to Ag).
example: Ethanal + Tollens' → silver mirror forms; ethanoic acid is the organic product. Ag⁺ + e⁻ → Ag.

definition: Caused by dissolved Ca²⁺ and Mg²⁺ ions in water. Hard water does not lather easily with soap. Temporary hardness is caused by Ca(HCO₃)₂ and can be removed by boiling. Permanent hardness is caused by CaSO₄ and requires ion exchange or washing soda.
example: Ca(HCO₃)₂ → CaCO₃ + H₂O + CO₂ when boiled — this removes temporary hardness.

definition: Chlorine or chlorine compounds are added to water to kill pathogenic bacteria and microorganisms, making it safe to drink.
example: Cl₂ + H₂O → HCl + HOCl; HOCl is the active disinfecting agent.

definition: The trapping of heat by atmospheric gases (mainly CO₂, CH₄, H₂O vapour) which absorb infrared radiation emitted from the Earth's surface, warming the planet.
example: Increased CO₂ from burning fossil fuels enhances the greenhouse effect, causing global warming.

definition: Rain with a pH below normal (~5.6) due to dissolved SO₂ and NOₓ gases from burning fossil fuels. Damages ecosystems, buildings, and metals.
example: SO₂ + H₂O → H₂SO₃; further oxidised to H₂SO₄ — forms sulfuric acid rain.

definition: The thinning of the ozone layer (O₃) in the stratosphere, primarily caused by chlorofluorocarbons (CFCs) releasing Cl atoms that catalytically destroy ozone.
example: Cl + O₃ → ClO + O₂; ClO + O → Cl + O₂ — Cl is regenerated (catalytic cycle).

definition: One mole of any substance contains 6.0 × 10²³ particles (Avogadro's constant). Moles = mass ÷ molar mass. At RTP, one mole of gas occupies 24.0 L.
example: 2.0 g of H₂: moles = 2.0 ÷ 2 = 1.0 mol; volume at RTP = 1.0 × 24.0 = 24.0 L.
